source.title: Scanning the active center of formolase to identify key residues for enhanced C1 to C3 bioconversion

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Formolase (FLS) is a computationally designed enzyme that catalyzes carboligation of formaldehyde into C2 glycolaldehyde (GA) or C3 dihydroxyacetone (DHA), but its application is limited by low catalytic activity and poor DHA selectivity. Previous directed evolution improved activity but missed potentially beneficial residues. This study aims to systematically scan the active center to identify key residues for enhanced C1 to C3 bioconversion, using single-site saturation mutagenesis of 25 residues within 8 Å of the active center, screening ~5,000 variants via a DHA-specific colorimetric assay, and characterizing beneficial variants kinetically and structurally.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: FLS catalyzes carboligation of formaldehyde to GA or DHA but has low activity and product promiscuity. 25 residues within 8 Å of the active center were screened by single-point saturation mutagenesis. A colorimetric DHA assay identified variants with improved DHA formation, notably S236F with 7.6-fold increase. MD simulations suggest S236F increases local hydrophobicity, favoring DHA formation. Study provides improved variants and insights for rational engineering.
process_signals: Problem: low catalytic activity and product promiscuity of FLS.
Approach: systematic scanning of active-center residues via saturation mutagenesis.
Key result: S236F variant shows 7.6-fold DHA improvement.
Mechanistic insight: increased hydrophobicity predisposes intermediate to nucleophilic attack.
methods: Single-point saturation mutagenesis of 25 residues within 8 Å of active center.
Colorimetric screening assay specific for DHA.
metrics: Fold improvement in DHA formation (7.6-fold for S236F).

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: FLS is key for artificial C1 bioconversion but limited by low activity and low DHA preference. Previous directed evolution improved activity up to 9.2-fold but may miss beneficial mutations due to library size limitations. This study uses scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 active-center residues to identify key residues, complementing directed evolution.
process_signals: Initial problem: low catalytic activity and poor DHA selectivity of FLS.
Constraints: previous directed evolution may not cover all beneficial mutations.
Hypothesis: residues within 8 Å of active center affect DHA formation and can be optimized.
Rationale: systematic scanning can identify missed beneficial sites.
methods: Comparison to previous directed evolution results.
failures_or_limits: Directed evolution library size impractical to cover all mutations.

section_gists.heading: Screening of FLS variants with improved DHA formation from single-site saturation mutation libraries
section_gists.gist: 25 residues within 8 Å of active center were selected for single-point saturation mutagenesis. ~200 transformants per site screened (~5,000 total) using DHA-specific colorimetric assay. 10 substitutions at 6 sites improved DHA 1.5-5.3-fold in whole-cell biocatalysis. Seven variants with >2-fold improvement (S26F, G109I, S236F, S236L, H281Y, C398M, C398A) were re-tested with replicates, confirming improvement. Some sites (S26F, H281Y) overlapped with previous directed evolution; new sites (G109I, S236F/L, C398M/A) were identified. Highlights complementarity of scanning saturation mutagenesis to directed evolution.
process_signals: Experimental design: 25 residues selected based on distance from active center.
Screening: ~200 transformants per site, colorimetric DHA assay.
Initial hits: 10 substitutions at 6 sites with 1.5-5.3-fold improvement.
Validation: seven variants with >2-fold improvement re-tested with biological replicates.
Key observation: identification of new beneficial sites not found by directed evolution.
Reason for approach: directed evolution library size impractical; scanning ensures coverage.
methods: Colorimetric DHA quantification assay (no GA interference).
PCR and DNA sequencing of hits.
metrics: Fold improvement in DHA formation (1.5-5.3-fold initial; >2-fold validated).
failures_or_limits: No biological replicates in initial screening of ~5,000 transformants.
Variations in improvement levels between initial and re-test rounds.

section_gists.heading: Kinetic characterization of selected FLS variants
section_gists.gist: Seven variants with >2-fold DHA improvement were purified and kinetically characterized. Except C398A, all showed 1.4-2.4-fold increased affinity (lower Km) for formaldehyde. kcat values slightly decreased or similar. Catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km) improved up to 1.6-fold. High affinity is important due to formaldehyde toxicity and diffusion limitations. Trade-off: improved affinity but not always higher turnover.
process_signals: Kinetic results: increased substrate affinity (lower Km) for most variants.
Trade-off: kcat not significantly improved; C398A lost affinity improvement.
Interpretation: high affinity beneficial for intracellular formaldehyde limitation.
Connection to previous work: consistent with H281Y/S26F combination improving affinity.
methods: Kinetic assay using glycerol dehydrogenase (reacts with both GA and DHA).
failures_or_limits: C398A showed no improvement in affinity or catalytic efficiency.
Kinetic assay uses glycerol dehydrogenase, which cannot distinguish DHA from GA, so kcat reflects total carboligation activity.

section_gists.heading: Determination of the product preference of selected FLS variants
section_gists.gist: HPLC analysis of reaction products revealed DHA formation and DHA/GA preference. S236F showed 7.6-fold higher DHA and 2.6-fold higher DHA/GA ratio than WT. Other variants also improved DHA except S26F. Discrepancy with kinetic constants due to glycerol dehydrogenase assay measuring total activity. MD simulations: S236F increases local hydrophobicity, predisposing intermediate to third formaldehyde attack. H281Y forms hydrogen bond stabilizing intermediate. Reshaping active-site hydrophobicity is key for C1 to C3 conversion.
process_signals: Product analysis: HPLC to distinguish DHA and GA.
Key result: S236F best for DHA formation and selectivity.
Mechanistic insight: MD simulations show increased hydrophobicity and hydrogen bonding effects.
Design revision: understanding residue roles (S236, H281) guides future combinatorial engineering.
Partial result: S26F alone not effective for DHA, contrary to previous combination studies.
methods: HPLC analysis of enzymatic reaction products.
metrics: Fold improvement in DHA: 7.6-fold for S236F.
failures_or_limits: S26F single mutation not effective for DHA formation.
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme DHA activities, possibly due to expression variations or reaction conditions.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Systematic scanning of 25 active-center residues identified new beneficial mutations for DHA formation and formaldehyde affinity. Top variant S236F produced 7.4-fold higher DHA. Mutations can be combined with each other or with previous directed evolution hits. Study provides mechanistic guidance for further engineering of FLS for C1 bioconversion.
process_signals: Summary: new sites and substitutions identified.
Future direction: combinatorial mutagenesis of identified mutations.
Limitation: single-site mutations only; need for combinatorial approaches.
Implication: mechanistic insights (hydrophobicity, hydrogen bonding) guide engineering.
metrics: 7.4-fold higher DHA from formaldehyde for S236F.
failures_or_limits: Only single-site mutations tested; combined effects unknown.
Potential trade-offs in enzyme stability or expression not addressed.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study follows a linear trajectory: problem identification (low activity and DHA selectivity) → hypothesis (active-center residues influence DHA formation) → systematic scanning via saturation mutagenesis → screening and identification of beneficial variants → kinetic and product preference characterization → mechanistic explanation via MD simulations → conclusions and future directions.
The research complements previous directed evolution by covering missed beneficial mutations, emphasizing the value of systematic active-site scanning.
The key insight is that reshaping active-site hydrophobicity, particularly via S236F, enhances DHA formation by favoring the third formaldehyde attack.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the identified single mutations can be combined additively or synergistically to further improve activity and selectivity.
The effect of mutations on enzyme stability, expression, or other properties not measured.
The generalizability of the hydrophobicity mechanism to other FLS variants or related enzymes.
The exact role of C398, as C398A lost activity while C398M improved affinity; the text does not fully explain this difference.

events.summary: FLS has low catalytic activity and product promiscuity, limiting its application.
FLS exhibits a low preference for the C3 product DHA.
Residues within 8 Å of the active center affect DHA formation and can be optimized.
Scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 active-center residues was used instead of directed evolution.
Approximately 5,000 variants were screened using a DHA-specific colorimetric assay.
Seven amino acid substitutions beneficial for DHA formation were identified.
S236F mutation improved DHA formation by 7.6-fold.
MD simulations suggested S236F increases local hydrophobicity, favoring DHA generation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains abstract and background, clearly stating problem, hypothesis, design, and key results.

events.summary: Seven variants with >2-fold improvement were re-tested with biological replicates, confirming higher DHA formation activity.
New beneficial sites (G109I, S236F/L, C398M/A) were identified that were not found by previous directed evolution, complementing the two strategies.
Kinetic characterization showed most variants had 1.4- to 2.4-fold increased affinity (lower Km) for formaldehyde, but kcat slightly decreased or similar.
MD simulations suggested S236F mutation increases local hydrophobicity, predisposing intermediate to nucleophilic attack by third formaldehyde.
S26F single mutation was not effective for DHA formation, contrary to previous combination studies.
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme DHA activities possibly due to expression variations or reaction conditions.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key experimental results: screening hits, kinetic characterization, product preference analysis, MD simulations, and identified limitations.
This chunk contains only reference citations and a publisher's note, with no research process events.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Formolase (FLS) has low catalytic activity and poor DHA selectivity, limiting its application in C1 to C3 bioconversion.

canonical_events.summary: FLS has low catalytic activity and product promiscuity, limiting its application.
FLS exhibits a low preference for the C3 product DHA.
Residues within 8 Å of the active center affect DHA formation and can be optimized.
Scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 active-center residues was used instead of directed evolution to ensure coverage.
Approximately 5,000 variants were screened using a DHA-specific colorimetric assay, identifying 10 beneficial substitutions at 6 sites.
Seven variants with >2-fold improvement were re-tested with biological replicates, confirming higher DHA formation activity.
New beneficial sites (G109I, S236F/L, C398M/A) were identified that were not found by previous directed evolution, complementing the two strategies.
Kinetic characterization showed most variants had 1.4- to 2.4-fold increased affinity (lower Km) for formaldehyde, but kcat slightly decreased or similar.
key_numbers: kcat/Km: WT 0.82 min^-1 mM^-1, variants 0.87-1.34 min^-1 mM^-1

canonical_events.summary: S236F mutation improved DHA formation by 7.6-fold and DHA/GA ratio by 2.6-fold, making it the top variant.
MD simulations suggested S236F increases local hydrophobicity, predisposing intermediate to nucleophilic attack by third formaldehyde, favoring DHA generation.
S26F single mutation was not effective for DHA formation, contrary to previous combination studies.
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme DHA activities possibly due to expression variations or reaction conditions.
Only single-site mutations tested; combined effects unknown, and potential trade-offs in enzyme stability or expression not addressed.

edges.reason: Both describe the initial problem: low activity and poor DHA selectivity.
The problem of low DHA selectivity motivates the hypothesis that active-center residues can be optimized.
The hypothesis leads to the design of scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 residues.
The design is implemented by screening ~5,000 variants.
Initial hits are validated with biological replicates.
Validation confirms new beneficial sites not found by directed evolution.
Kinetic data leads to product preference analysis, highlighting S236F.
The outstanding performance of S236F motivates MD simulations to explain the mechanism.
S26F was among validated variants but later found ineffective for DHA formation alone.
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme activities is noted after validation.
After mechanistic insights, limitations of single-site mutations and unaddressed trade-offs are acknowledged.

must_cover_points: Low catalytic activity and poor DHA selectivity of FLS.
Hypothesis that active-center residues affect DHA formation.
Design of scanning saturation mutagenesis of 25 residues.
Screening of ~5,000 variants and identification of beneficial substitutions.
Validation of top variants with biological replicates.
Identification of new beneficial sites not found by directed evolution.
Kinetic characterization showing increased substrate affinity.
S236F as top variant with 7.6-fold DHA improvement.
MD simulations explaining S236F mechanism via increased hydrophobicity.
Failure of S26F single mutation for DHA formation.
Discrepancy between whole-cell and purified enzyme activities.
Limitation of single-site mutations and need for combinatorial approaches.
